Looking at the code, the merge logic has a bug in the `else` branch (when `left[i] == right[j]`). The function is supposed to merge two sorted lists preserving all elements (like merge-sort), but when equal elements are found, it appends both elements (lines 69-70), which would create duplicates. However, the docstring says it's a dedup merge, suggesting duplicates should be deduplicated.

But wait - let me re-read the docstring: "The merge preserves all elements from both lists in sorted order, analogous to the merge step of merge-sort." So it should keep both.

The real bug is lines 76-77: there's an extra post-processing step that removes the last element if it equals both lists' last elements. This is incorrect logic that arbitrarily removes an element from the merged result.

Additionally, lines 69-70 append both copies of equal elements (creating duplicates), while lines 76-77 try to fix one case but in a broken way.

The core issue: when `left[i] == right[j]`, the function appends both (lines 69-70), creating a duplicate. A proper merge should only append one of them (dedup behavior) or both (preserve-all behavior). The function name says "dedup_merge" suggesting deduplication, so line 70 (`merged.append(right[j])`) is wrong - it should not append the duplicate. And lines 76-77 are a broken attempt to fix this.
